data.title: When Geopolitics Becomes An Economic Input
data.description:
Markets have long struggled to price geopolitical risk. Part of the issue is that each flare-up tends to be viewed as a one-off volatility jolt to be weathered and then faded once there is resolution.

Today, however, geopolitical risk is no longer a series of isolated events. It’s a defining feature of a more fragmented, multipolar world (for more, see our 2025 Secular Outlook, “The Fragmentation Era”). Multinational institutions no longer function as reliably as they once did. Stability has eroded.

Perhaps more than ever, geopolitics directly shapes trade flows, supply chains, industrial policy, energy security, defense spending, fiscal policy, inflation, and growth. In other words, geopolitics is now an essential economic input. As a result, it is an increasingly important input into investment decisions as well.

Passive investment strategies were well-suited to a world of low geopolitical risk, central bank balance sheet abundance, and compressed volatility. That world is gone. In its place is an environment that distributes risk unevenly – creating distinct winners and losers across countries, sectors, and asset classes.

Navigating that dispersion requires flexibility: the ability to rotate, adjust, and exploit dislocations that a passive index, by construction, cannot. In our view, agile, multi‑sector approaches are best positioned for this environment, where it will be critically important to price risk objectively. Successful investors will need to discern across multiple sectors and security types, as well as liquidity profiles, to choose the best risk-adjusted opportunities to deliver returns.

Start with the initial conditions

It’s tempting to view recent conflict-driven volatility through the lens of early 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine. That year’s energy shock hit a post-pandemic global economy marked by pent-up imbalances, while bond yields were near all-time lows. As inflation surged, stocks and bonds sold off together, correlations spiked across asset classes, and diversification appeared to vanish just when it was needed.

The defining difference between then and now is the starting point of yields. Bond yields are much higher today than in 2022, providing a far greater cushion for investors. As a result, even during recent stress, fixed income has demonstrated a greater ability to absorb shocks.

Figure 1: Yields have provided greater cushion than in the comparable 2022 period

Lean into dispersion

Geopolitical volatility doesn’t affect all countries or asset classes equally. Broader market performance, or beta, matters less when winners and losers are increasingly distinct.

Geopolitical fragmentation rewards selectivity. This can be observed today within emerging markets, for example, where the lines of differentiation and resilience are increasingly drawn between energy exporters versus importers (for more, see our March 2026 Cyclical Outlook, “Layered Uncertainty: Conflict, Credit Stress, and AI”).

Targeted tools for inflation hedging

Since the Middle East conflict began, inflation has been a foremost market concern, highlighting the role that inflation-linked bonds can play in portfolios. For example, in the U.S., Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) can be an effective tool. However, they also draw occasional criticism during volatile periods: when real (inflation-adjusted) yields rise, longer-dated TIPS prices can still fall due to interest rate risk even as inflation risks increase.

Approaches that emphasize shorter-dated inflation-linked bond exposures and more targeted tools such as inflation swaps can enhance the inflation-hedging properties in portfolios. This can better isolate inflation mitigation while managing sensitivity to yield movements.

Look for opportunities in commodities

Over the past couple of months, one asset class behaved largely as theory would suggest: commodities, which have seen large price swings and often sharp gains as inflation expectations rose.

Importantly, outsize short-term volatility tends to reset risk premia, or the amount of compensation investors receive for specific risks. In commodities, this has created tremendous longer-term opportunities, particularly for investors focused on relative value – for example, across commodity curves and cross-commodity relationships – rather than directional bets.

Conclusion: Building resilience for an age of geopolitical volatility

To strengthen resilience, investors should construct portfolios to adapt to both episodic shocks as well as structural shifts in a more geopolitically driven market environment.

Over short time horizons, particularly during bouts of stress, correlations across asset classes can appear skewed and diversification may seem elusive. Yet, when portfolios are built for long-term resilience, diversification can reassert itself.

Flexible multi-sector strategies – dynamic bond funds and income-oriented portfolios – are built for precisely today’s regime. These strategies can lean into dispersion, pursue targeted inflation mitigation, and capture relative value.

This is a world that rewards the adaptive and penalizes the rigid. Flexibility isn’t just a feature of good portfolio construction today. It is the strategy.

Disclosures

Past performance is not a guarantee or a reliable indicator of future results.

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed. Commodities contain heightened risk, including market, political, regulatory and natural conditions, and may not be appropriate for all investors. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic and industry conditions. Investing in foreign-denominated and/or -domiciled securities may involve heightened risk due to currency fluctuations, and economic and political risks, which may be enhanced in emerging markets. Inflation-linked bonds (ILBs) issued by a government are fixed income securities whose principal value is periodically adjusted according to the rate of inflation; ILBs decline in value when real interest rates rise. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) are ILBs issued by the U.S. government.

Statements concerning financial market trends are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. This material is distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed.

data.title: ServiceNow Q1: Why The 24x P/E Is A Mirage
data.description:
Reading ServiceNow, Inc.'s (NOW) Q1 earnings release, initially, I could not understand the reason for the stock being down 13% in after-hours. The result was not that bad; the valuation at first glance seems reasonable (P/E non-GAAP), and even in operational terms and AI-related initiatives, ServiceNow is improving.

But going deeper, it is possible to understand why the market is more cautious. And the reason is not exclusively due to disruption by AI; it also has to do with valuation and with the growth expectations that the market previously had.

ServiceNow Q1: Pretty Solid

The snapshot summarizes what I mentioned about ServiceNow’s Q1. It was very solid; it is difficult to understand the reason the stock is falling. Revenue was a slight beat, and adjusted EPS was in line with estimates.

But note also that Q2 guidance was for subscription revenue of $3.8 billion, equivalent to 22% YoY growth, as well as full-year guidance of $15.7 billion, above consensus and confirming that growth for 2026 should remain above 22%.

The rest of the highlights were also positive. Remaining performance obligations (RPO) stand at $25 billion and grew 25%, and adjusted operating margin expanded 100 bps. It is worth mentioning that the current RPO also continues growing and is more than $12 billion; that is, it is revenue that the company expects to recognize in the next quarters.

The customer cohort chart also shows that ServiceNow continues to be a very healthy company. Not only can we see new customers in the chart, but also a clear trend that the longer the relationship, the greater the contract value with NOW. Even though this is not something that guarantees there will be no disruption, it indicates that the company really manages to work the relationship with its partners, manages to add value, and manages to upsell/cross-sell. In fact, another metric that proves this is the number of customers with a $5M annual contract value, which went from 602 in Q4 2025 to 630 in Q1. Not to mention the renewal rate that remains at 97%.

ServiceNow Presentation

In addition, the company also highlighted other operational points, such as the launch of new features, such as an AI-native experience capable of building agents, as well as the launch of Autonomous Workforce, which is also related to AI, as NOW explains:

“Autonomous Workforce, a new class of AI specialists that execute enterprise jobs end-to-end with built-in governance and human oversight. The first available out-of-the-box is a Level 1 Service Desk AI Specialist that autonomously diagnoses and resolves common IT support requests.”

In short, here in Q1, we basically have everything that a long-term shareholder wants to see: operational. Still solid. Revenue growth? Very good. Margins? Good enough.

2 Negative Points For ServiceNow

Up to this point, I believe you might be thinking that I am bullish on ServiceNow and that my rating would be a buy.

But no. I am not optimistic about ServiceNow for two reasons.

The first is uncertainty. Even though the result today is solid, this does not guarantee that in the next 10 years it will be. We have to consider that competition may advance substantially, since even with a switching cost moat, technology is something that is evolving a lot, and we have several other SaaS companies that are behemoths and are developing AI agents and increasingly complete platforms to compete directly and indirectly with ServiceNow. In addition, partnerships in this sector are always seen with some nuance; a partner today can be a competitor in 5 years, as is the case with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT).

But of course, here I am precisely talking about uncertainty. I am not trying to predict that Microsoft will take market share from ServiceNow in 5 or 10 years; maybe that never happens. But we cannot act as if the moat here were so solid that competition is something of little concern. Competition from new players and existing players that are investing massively in R&D and developing both new alternatives and improving their respective platforms.

In addition, we also have the case of AI agents as a mixed point in NOW’s case. Now it may be something to increase ACV, a new product for its partnerships, so the trend that contracts continue increasing and ServiceNow continues to be a relevant partner for its customers is strong. But at the same time, it is difficult to say how this will be in 5-10 years. For instance, could an AI agent on ServiceNow’s platform reduce the need for seats? Causing something close to cannibalization. So even if there is still pricing power due to moats, there may be a reduction in contract volume from new customers, or at least it is an uncertainty that the company needs to worry about.

All these risks are, in fact, risks that I would be willing to take; after all, every equity has uncertainties. But then comes my second point, valuation.

One of the reasons for the stock falling, in my view, is that the market did not want just a slight beat; it wanted the company to prove that demand is red-hot or growing much more than consensus. And before, expectations were much higher, but even though the stock has fallen more than 30% in the past 12 months, the valuation remains rich.

In my opinion, the forward P/E of ~24x is misleading. The software industry standard is to have high stock-based compensation (SBC). For ServiceNow, it is no different. In Q1, non-GAAP net income was $1 billion. But this was after excluding $558 million of SBC; GAAP net income is $469 million.

Doing the math with GAAP net income, Q1 annualized goes to something close to $1.87 billion. This would give us a P/E of 49x. The consensus is a forward 43x P/E GAAP.

It is not something that completely invalidates the case. If the top line continues growing 22% for 5 years, and operating leverage also affects SBC (with the company keeping this expense controlled), we can reach very reasonable multiples through compounding/growth. But note that treating NOW stock as a stock that trades at a P/E of 40x-50x already completely changes the narrative.

Now we are talking about a stock that is doing well but has some technological uncertainties, and much of this growth is already priced in through a high P/E.

Bottom Line

ServiceNow is a stock worth having on the radar, and I believe Q1 was solid. But I do not think the valuation, together with the uncertainties, allows me to be bullish on the company. Therefore, my rating is a hold.

data.title: Safe Bulkers' Valuation Margin Of Safety Is Starting To Shrink
data.description:
Almost three months after the publication of my previous coverage, Safe Bulkers, Inc. (SB) has already delivered approximately 20% returns and justified my buy rating. For over a year of analyzing SB, it has continuously increased. And I believe that is justifiable considering its robust performance, strategic operations, and ample liquidity amid macroeconomic volatility. But at this point, I believe that SB has already reached what I expected of it. Valuation is already a bit stretched, while technicals show early bearish signals.

Safe Bulkers: Growth Has Resumed, But Mounting Headwinds Caught Up With It

Shippers, including dry bulk carriers, are highly sensitive to external factors, such as macroeconomic volatility, geopolitical tensions, energy commodity price swings, and tariff wars. After all, they weaken the purchasing power and production capacity of consumers and producers. The impact of these continued to manifest amid the rising prices, and even a known shipper like Safe Bulkers, Inc. could not avoid it. However, market dynamics can offset them, supported by SB’s operational strengths and vessel management. This was evident in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $72.6M, up by 1.5% YoY from $71.5M. This was a slight improvement from my previous coverage, considering the revenue decrease of 3.7% YoY then. This could tell us that SB has already started to cope with the changing market dynamics. As you can see, its time charter equivalent increased to $17,050 versus $16,521 YoY. This means that each vessel or ship generated higher rates per day. This should not be surprising in line with the modest increase in the Baltic Dry Index, or BDI. I think lower oil prices helped because that meant lower fuel expenses for SB and lower transportation costs for customers. So, the demand for grain, coal, and iron ore improved. Another factor was the slight improvement in China’s economy as GDP increased. After all, this meant increased economic activity, both on the production and consumption side. And because it is known as the top steel producer, this meant more iron ore imports and industrial activity. In turn, demand for dry bulk shippers also rose and pushed dry bulk rates up.

However, the impact of the mounting cost pressures has already caught up with SB. This happened despite having fewer vessels and operating days. Even the sustained decrease in oil and fuel prices did not help lower its expenses. Somehow, I understand this due to the increased activity in the dry bulk market. More demand means more routes but also entails more expenses. Another factor to consider is stubborn inflation. For instance, inflation in the US is still above the 2.0% target, so prices are still increasing at rates higher than the desired level. Also, geopolitical tensions affected routes and increased insurance/risk claims of vessel crews. As a result, the operating costs and expenses offset revenue growth. Nonetheless, revenues were still high enough to cover all costs and expenses, showing that SB continued to generate enough to sustain its operations despite potential market disruptions. In the end, its operating margin remained positive and decent at 25.8% despite its notable decrease from 34.4% YoY.

Middle East Tension: The Good And The Bad

Although SB’s operations are not tied to energy commodities, the Middle East tension will most likely impact it due to several reasons.

On the positive side, this can affect routes and even tighten dry bulk shipper supply. Considering this, the number of available vessels will decrease, which can push shipping rates up across the market. Because demand outpaces supply, SB can also capture more demand from dry bulk importers/exporters as they switch to more reliable shippers. It can also capture shipping reroutes considering its wide global exposure. Another thing is that some of its vessels have a spot market exposure, so higher spot rates mean a higher amount earned by SB. But considering the situation, these are mostly effects in the short run.

On the negative side, the tension has already sent oil prices to their one-year highs. This means higher fuel expenses for SB. Its vessel staff or crew may ask for higher compensation and war risk premiums, leading to higher vessel expenses. And if the tension escalates, global trade slowdown may take place in the long run. Wild oil price swings and sustained inflation may slow down economic activity, including infrastructure and industrial. With that, demand for iron ore and coal may also decrease in the long run.

Risk Mitigants

Right now, we can’t accurately predict the extent of the impact of the Middle East tension because it will most likely extend to multiple industries. On a lighter note, I believe that SB has several risk mitigants to protect its business.

One factor to consider is its business model. The majority of its vessels employ a period charter type. These are mostly under a contract or agreement for a specific period. This may protect SB from rate fluctuations. Also, it is guaranteed to derive revenues based on its agreements or contracts with its customers.

Another factor to consider is its young fleet age of 10.39 years. Although some of its ships were built before 2010, many of the new ones were built in 2022-2025. Additionally, it has new vessels under its order book, where four will be delivered within the year, two next year, one in 2028, and one in 2029. Having younger vessels means more efficient sailing with less risk of repairs and maintenance. Also, this makes it SB suitable for reroutes or longer travel, making it more reliable for many customers.

Lastly, robust liquidity reveals that SB can survive market headwinds. For instance, its cash and cash equivalents remain ample at $128.4M. This is more than enough to cover borrowings maturing within the year and other current liabilities. SB complements it with prudent debt management. Note that its maturity profile is still long, like in my previous coverage. If you look at its maturity table, about 25% of the amount has a maturity of five years and longer.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I updated the DCF model. Because SB’s actual revenue beat my expectations, FCFF also beat my estimation. If you remember, my expected revenue high and low were $271M and $266M in my previous coverage. However, SB generated $275.7M, lower than in 2024 but quite better than my expected amount.

Meanwhile, I still set its perpetual growth rate at 2.00% to be consistent with my previous coverage. This is also in line with the US target inflation of 2.00%. On the other hand, I derived WACC using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 5.0%, in line with the market standard.

I set its Beta at 1.00 in line with the S&P 500. Lastly, I updated its cash and debt based on the most recent report. With all this information, my new TP or target price, is $6.10. This is higher than the TP in my previous coverage, which justifies the recent price uptrend. However, the current price has already exceeded it. With that, SB is already fully priced, which makes entry at this price risky.

I also used price ratios to assess SB. P/B shows that the stock trades at 0.80x its book value, which is already higher than the five-year average of 0.56x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $4.55. Meanwhile, P/CPS using cash per share tells a different story. Cash per share has increased over the years, which also justified the price rally of SB. Using this, it derives a TP of $8.38. Now, you can see that the two financial metrics disagree with one another. To be conservative, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

SB’s technicals are still generally bearish. As the price continues to rebound from the dip last month. It also trends above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which shows that recent market sentiments have remained optimistic. However, you can clearly see that the price uptrend appears weaker than in early 2026. This is probably due to the impact of the Middle East tension. And I will not blame the investors since it can be disruptive.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI warrant caution. You can see the opposite movement between the increasing price highs and decreasing highs of the two indicators on March 4 and April 20. This shows early bearish divergence signals due to the fact that fewer buyers entered as the stock became more expensive. Additionally, the MACD line is decreasing below the signal line, while the histogram is within negative territory. This implies decreasing buying volume, increasing selling volume, and that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Lastly, RSI shows that the weakening momentum is due to recent overbuying. With that, investors must be more cautious today.

Conclusion

Safe Bulkers, Inc. remains well-positioned with its prudent vessel management and strategic exposure to spot rates. Its liquidity also supports its sustainability and dividend longevity. However, these may not be enough to make explosive growth, considering that market dynamics are becoming more volatile. Valuation and technicals also suggest caution since it’s already fully priced and has been overbought. I’m downgrading my rating on this stock from buy to hold.

Market Overview

The first quarter of 2026 was a volatile one. It was dominated by constantly shifting market factors, heightened geopolitical risk, and the related fear of inflation from rising energy prices. The S&P 500 Index (SP500) finished down 4.3%. This doesn't sound too terrible given the market's strength for the last three years and that we are currently in the middle of a war. During the early parts of the quarter, value significantly outperformed growth for the first time in a while. The Russell 1000® Value Index was up 2.1% while the Russell 1000® Growth Index was down 9.8%. We also saw a return of market breadth as the S&P 500® Equal Weight Index was up 0.7% while the Magnificent Seven was down a combined 11.2%. Finally, the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index (VIX) finished the quarter at its highest level since Liberation Day last year, showing that investors are feeling a high level of concern.

The fear of inflation left its mark on the financials and information technology sectors. Both were leaders for the last two years, but this quarter they were the market's two worst-performing areas. In tech, the concern around rising interest rates led investors to question whether the large hyperscalers could cut back on spending and what effect that would have on the supply chain, specifically within semiconductors and hardware. Software stocks also felt the pain when the release of Anthropic's new large language model (LLM) raised questions about which companies could be rendered obsolete by artificial intelligence (AI).

While these market concerns are certainly valid, economic data and the underlying earnings of companies have remained strong. This is likely the reason the market has held up okay.

Forward S&P 500 earnings are projected to rise 15% in 2026 over 2025, leaving the market trading at a price-to-earnings ratio under 20x for the first time since 2023.

Portfolio Review

Top securities

Corning's (GLW) positive performance was tied to a strong second-quarter earnings result and forward guidance supporting a continuation of those trends. The company's fiber optics and related technologies are integral in building AI infrastructure across the globe. We believe Corning should see market growth across several key products for the next three to five years.

Chevron (CVX) shares contributed to the quarter's performance due to the war in the Middle East sending commodity prices of crude oil and natural gas, to multi-year highs. We estimate that 10% to 15% of total supply could be removed from the global market for an extended period. Chevron has significant exposure to spot commodity prices and is expected to benefit immediately from a higher price regime in its upstream energy segment. Also, the company is positioned to benefit from improved refining margins. Chevron shares reflect improved earnings and cash flow from this event.

Williams shares benefitted from the conflict in the Middle East, which sent commodity prices of crude oil and natural gas to multi-year highs. We estimate that 10% to 15% of total supply could be removed from the global market for an extended period. Williams has limited commodity exposure, but the company should benefit from higher volumes moving through its vast pipeline system. The US natural gas market is incrementally viewed across the world as the most secure source. Williams is well placed to benefit from any improved volumes in the liquefied natural gas (LNG) export market.

Analog Devices (ADI) pushed toward new all-time highs after solid earnings gave investors confidence that the analog cycle is now beyond its bottom. We believe the cycle is on track for an early recovery in 2026.

AT&T reported strong fourth-quarter guidance and continued to execute its long-term strategy steadily. The company's fiber positioning remains strong, and management signaled a large stock buyback for 2026, which displays massive confidence in the balance sheet.

Bottom securities

Microsoft delivered weak quarterly performance, driven by industry-wide concerns over a slowdown in software seat licenses, a key worry among investors.

Abbott (ABT) shares underperformed due to disappointing fourth-quarter 2025 financial results and softer than expected 2026 sales guidance. Abbott's nutrition business, is sensitive to consumer spending trends and was the primary source of weakness. Abbott's company-specific issues were compounded by deteriorating investor sentiment across the medical device industry, which underperformed within the healthcare sector in the first quarter.

Broadcom (AVGO) was weak for the quarter as higher inflation led investors to fear a slowdown in hyperscaler spending. This led to lower spending on custom silicon, the main reason for Broadcom's strength over the last couple of years. We believe Broadcom still has some of the best tech in the space and will be one of the biggest winners from AI.

IBM (IBM) shares pulled back as a result of weaker than expected quarterly earnings. Revenue growth in the key software business did not meet elevated expectations. We believe that IBM is key in the AI marketplace, with good prospects for consistent growth over the next several years.

Accenture (ACN) delivered weaker performance during the quarter. Investor concerns rose about decelerating revenue growth due to government contract cancellations and softening demand for discretionary information technology (IT) spending. The uncertain net effect of AI fueled these fears. While fiscal fourth-quarter financials demonstrated that these fears were inflated, we believe the company needs to execute on its 2026 guidance before sentiment fully recovers.

Outlook

Forecasting a macroeconomic outlook in this current market might be the most difficult task we have faced in recent memory. The S&P 500 Index is coming off strong double-digit returns for three straight years while uncertainty from geopolitical risk has cast a dark cloud over the market. From an economic standpoint, data appears to be solid, benefits from the One Big Beautiful Bill should start to show up very soon, and tax refunds are expected to be higher than in previous years.

Inflation data has been encouraging lately, but we believe the market is not fully prepared for the possibility of rising interest rates if energy prices stay elevated. Not that long ago, the market was anticipating further rate cuts in 2026. If the US Federal Reserve (Fed) moves in the opposite direction, that may spark major concerns about the economy going forward. More than ever, it appears the economy and the stock market are both tied to the theme of AI and the infrastructure buildout around it. Our concern is that hyperscaler spending could be much choppier than the market anticipates, creating an air pocket in earnings growth for many companies tied to this theme. Still, we are believers in AI for the long term.

We maintain that companies with strong balance sheets and solid earnings growth should find success in the long run, regardless of the macro picture. We will continue to use our fundamental research process to take a long-term approach and invest in stocks that we believe can do well irrespective of the macroeconomic backdrop.

Risk Considerations:

International investing presents specific risks, such as currency fluctuations, differences in financial accounting standards as well as potential political and economic instability.

Because the fund normally will hold a focused portfolio of stocks of fewer companies than many other diversified funds, the increase or decrease of the value of a single stock may have a greater impact on the fund's net asset value and total return.

As with all equity investing, there is the risk that an unexpected change in the market or within the company itself may have an adverse effect on its stock. The biggest risk of equity investing is that returns can fluctuate and investors can lose money.

There are risks associated with dividend investing, including that dividend-issuing companies may choose not to pay a dividend, may not have the ability to pay, or the dividend may be less than what is anticipated. Dividend issuing companies are subject to interest rate risk and high dividends can sometimes signal that a company is in distress.

Growth companies are expected to increase their earnings at a certain rate. When these expectations are not met, investors may punish the stocks excessively, even if earnings showed an absolute increase. Growth company stocks also typically lack the dividend yield that can cushion stock prices in market downturns. The companies engaged in the technology industry are subject to fierce competition and their products and services may be subject to rapid obsolescence. The values of these companies tend to fluctuate sharply.

Investments in mid-cap and small-cap companies generally involve greater risks than investing in larger capitalization companies. Mid-cap companies often have narrower commercial markets, more limited managerial and financial resources, and more volatile trading than larger, more established companies.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) may be affected by economic conditions including credit risk, interest rate risk and other factors that affect property values, rents or occupancies of real estate.

Indices

The Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index, or VIX, is a real-time market index that represents the market's expectation of 30-day forward-looking volatility. Derived from the price inputs of the S&P 500 index options, it provides a measure of market risk and investors' sentiments.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures a growth-oriented subset of the Russell 1000 Index, which tracks approximately 1,000 of the large-sized capitalization companies in the United States equities market.

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures a value-oriented subset of the Russell 1000® Index, which tracks approximately 1,000 of the large-sized capitalization companies in the U.S. equities market.

The S&P 500 Index - measures change in stock market conditions based on the average performance of 500 widely held common stocks. It is a market-weighted index calculated on a total return basis with dividends reinvested. The S&P 500 represents approximately 75% of the investable U.S. equity market.

The S&P 500® Equal Weight Index - the equal-weight version of the S&P 500. It includes the same constituents as the capitalization-weighted S&P 500, but each company in the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index is allocated a fixed weight, or 0.2% of the index total at each quarterly rebalance.

Indices are unmanaged, and one cannot invest directly in an index.
